name: Homework 1
description: Help me with the list.c and stack.c programs that my professor wants us to write

docs.content: The hwk1 is the file that is being made and it is calling the Stack.c and List.c files

CSE 101 -- Prog 1
Due before midnight, Saturday, April 26, 2025.
Late submissions will not be accepted/graded.

Changelog:
1. Objectives:
Use your List ADT and Stack ADT in an application.
2. Description:
Write a program to read and format text.
3. Details:
3.1 Inputs:

commands.in: which has the list of commands.
text.in: which has the input text to be formatted.
The goal is to read the commands.in file and perform the commands on the sample text input file (text.in).

Commands.in:
This consists of commands in each line. There are 4 types of commands:
open - which mentions the sample text input file(eg: text.in).
format - Which represents the alignment and line width of the output file.
undo - which request to undo the last "format" command.
exit - which will terminate the program.

Text.in:
This file consists of the input text which has to be formatted.

3.2 More details about the commands:

commands.in has the input commands that your program needs to process. Consider the inputs as stdin. So design your program to take inputs from stdin and when you execute your program, redirect commands.in. like: ./prog1 < commands.in

You can assume that inputs are well formed, however invalid commands may be given. For example, the user might inadvertently have a typo when entering a command. Your application needs to handle that gracefully e.g. with an error message.
Valid commands and what they do:

open <filename>
Input filename is formatted as "filename.in"", where filename is a string with only alphabets and no numericals or special characters.
Example: open text.in - This should open the text.in file as the input text file to be formatted. More details on handling input files are provided in section 3.5.
Words in the input text file are separated by a white space character.
format <F> <#>
F: represents the justification of the text in the new file; It can be either L(left align) or R(right align). Bonus: You can also implement F = C to center align the text on each line for bonus points(5 points).
#: A non-zero positive integer that represents the line width for each line in the new file.
When format command is encountered, your program should create a new file with the appropriate alignment(F) and the line width(#).
Example: format L 50 - This command signifies left alignment with 50 characters in each line.
format L 50 - should create a new file which Left aligns the text in the input file and fits only 50 characters in each line.
You don't need to split or hyphenate words. You can assume that all the words will fit in # spaces.
undo
Undo command should undo the action done by the previous "format" command. ie, delete the previously created file.
If multiple undo commands are issued continuously, then remove the output files in reverse order of their creation.
Undo operation can be implemented with a stack data structure. As "format" commands are executed, save the newly created filename into the stack using push operation. So at any given point, the stack should contain the filenames that are currently created and exist in the filesystem.
When an undo command is encountered, pop operation is used to identify the last created file by looking into the stack and the file is deleted from the filesystem.
Exit
Exit command is just the end of the program. You can exit the program once "exit" command is encountered in commands.in
3.3 Output:

We have two outputs.
stdout(standard output) - Which prints the message as commands are executed.
Output files - Which are the actual files that are generated/deleted based on the commands.
Stdout:
Print the message when you open a file, create a file, delete a file, for invalid command, and if "undo" cannot be executed. Sample output is shown below in Section 3.4.

Output files:

New file is created when the "format" command is executed. Filename should be "inputFilePrefix.Arg1Arg2".
Example: If the input file name is "text.in" and the format command is "format L 10", then the output filename should be "text.L10".
Undo command will delete the last created file. So after the undo command is executed there will be one less file in the filesystem.
One corner case to consider is, what if the stack is empty. Sample test case is provided below(case2). In this case just print a message in the standard output saying "Undo failed: format command stack is empty" and don't alter the filesystem.
Sample input is provided in text.in.

The expected output when asked to left justify the text using width of 70 i.e. the user command is: format L 70 is text.L70.
The expected output when asked to right justify the text using width of 50 i.e. the user command is: format R 50 is text.R50.
The expected output when asked to center the text using width of 45 i.e. the user command is: format C 45 is text.C45.
3.4 Sample test cases and outputs:

Case 1:
Sample Output Case 1
Case 2:
Sample Output Case 2
3.5 Additional Notes on Handling Input Files (text.in):

File Interaction:
Opening: Use fopen to open the file. Implement a prompt for users to input the filename.
Reading: Words are sequentially read using fscanf from the opened file.
Closing: Once all words are processed, fclose ensures the file is properly closed.
3.6 Running Your Program:

Sample Input: A sample command file named commands.in is provided.
Executing: Run your program ensuring it reads from stdin, like: ./prog1 < commands.in
Expected Output: Upon successful execution, the program should generate files such as text.L70, text.R50, and text.C45 based on the commands in the input file.
3.7 Files for this program:

Makefile: You don't touch this file. Running make should produce an executable called prog1.
List.h: You don't touch this file. Same as Hwk1.
List.c: This is from your Hwk1.
Stack.h: You don't touch this file. Same as Hwk1.
Stack.c: This is from your Hwk1.
formatter.c: This is where you write the application code.
4. Grading:
Rubric:

You start off with 100 points.
You lose credit for missing functionality, incorrect results,
poorly documented or formatted code, or not following instructions.
Particularly:

- test cases: 50 points, we have 5 test cases each has 10 points
- use of List ADT and Stack ADT functions: 30 points.
- style and commenting 20 points
- Bonus points: Get 5 points by implementing F = C to center the text on each line

Below is a partial list:

- up to 10 points off for inadequate comments or hard-to-read code
- up to 10 points off for not following instructions
- up to 10 points off for special handling to grade your homework (usually
  because you did not check that it runs on the unix timeshare server first).
- functionality points depending on importance

Make sure you:
a.  include a Makefile, .c files, and .h file
b.  generate an executable file called prog1 with your makefile
c.  have confirmed that it compiles and runs properly on unix.ucsc.edu
d.  followed the general instructions described in overview.html

5. Submission:
Make sure that you've compiled and tested your code on the campus unix timeshare before submitting.

Put your makefile and source files in a folder named prog1.

In the same directory where you have the prog1 folder, the following command will recursively archive the contents of your prog1 folder into a file named prog1.zip
zip -r prog1.zip prog1
Submit prog1.zip on Canvas under Program 1.
You can submit as often as you want up until the deadline. We will only look at your most recent submission.

name: Mid-quarter essay topic
description: Write a philosophical essay

docs.content:
are handed in late will be docked 5% per day. Essays will not be accepted more than 5
days after the deadline. So please make sure to hand the assignments in on time. If you
foresee any difficulties in submitting your essay on time, contact your TA before the due
date to request an extension.
Your essay will be evaluated according to the rubric in the last page.
TOPIC: Choose two philosophers from the first three weeks of the course (Plato, Albert
Camus, or Thomas Nagel) compare their views on the question of what gives meaning
to our lives. In your paper:
- Clearly explain each philosopher’s view.
- Compare and contrast their ideas—how are they similar or different?
- Argue for which view you find more convincing, and explain why.
Structure: Your paper should have a clear introduction, a body where you explain and
compare the views and develop your own position, and a brief conclusion that recaps
your argument (and, if you wish, reflects on its broader significance).
Tip: You don’t need to cover every aspect of each philosopher’s view. Focus on the
points you find most interesting or where you have the most to say. Use other aspects
only as needed to support your discussion. Feel free to use examples to clarify or
strengthen your argument.
Proper referencing:
- When you quote a sentence or a passage, i.e. if you use someone’s exact words, put
the passage in “ “ and put author’s name, the year, and the page number in parenthesis.
For Plato, instead of the page number, put the margin number in parenthesis.
e.g. “If different powers by nature deal with different things, and both opinion
and knowledge are powers but, as we claim, different ones, it follows from
these that the object of knowledge and the object of belief cannot be the
same” (Plato 2011, 478a-b).
- If you paraphrase an idea that you got from the texts, you must also put a parenthetical
reference.
e.g. Plato claims that the object of belief and knowledge are different (2011,
478a-b).

I want to use Camus' "the myth of Sisyphus" and Nagel's "the absurd" to say if life has no meaning then life's meaning must be created. 
-- I also want to bring up the fact that not having a meaning to life instead of being something that is depressing is instead liberating. The liberating aspect is the fact that it means that you are no longer bound to other responsibilities. You no longer have to live for others but instead can live for oneself.

name: Program 2
description: This is the second program that I am doing for my CSE101 class

docs.content:
Changelog:
1. Objectives:
Understand Preemptive priority CPU scheduling from Operating System context.
Implement Preemptive Priority CPU Scheduling using the priority_queue ADT from hwk2.
2. Description:
Simulate a preemptive priority-based CPU scheduling algorithm for a 2 CPU system(CPU0 and CPU1).

Preemptive scheduling is a CPU scheduling method where the operating system can interrupt a running process to assign the CPU to another process with higher priority. This ensures better system responsiveness and fairness in multitasking environments.

There will be a set of processes where each process is assigned a priority and the CPU should execute the process with the highest priority(lowest integer value) at any given point of time. All the currently available processes need to be available in the priority queue. If a new process with a higher priority arrives during the execution of a process, the currently executing process is preempted and added back to the queue. Make sure you schedule processes on both the CPUs(CPU0 and CPU1) whenever possible.

The simulation also should generate gantt charts that visually represent the scheduling timeline for both the CPUs.

The preemptive_scheduling.c file should be implemented using the functions from priority_queue.c and priority_queue.h provided in hwk2.
3. Details:
3.1 Inputs:

Process Details:
struct process is a structure that represents each process. Each process is a node in the priority queue. All the process details need to be added to an array of processes and passed to the simulate_preemptive_priority_scheduling function. This is done as part of the test.c file. Process is defined as a structure with following members:
pid: Unique Process ID.
arrival_time: The time at which the process arrives. Before the arrival time, the process is non-existent.
burst_time: Total CPU time required for the process to get executed fully.
remaining_time: CPU time left for completion. Initially the same as burst_time.
priority: Scheduling priority(lower value = higher priority). The highest priority is 0 and lowest priority is 20.
snapshot_times:
Snapshot times is an array of timestamps at which the simulate_preemptive_priority_scheduling function needs to print the current state of the priority queue and the gantt charts(CPU0 and CPU1).
3.2 Functions to be implemented:

Implements all functions declared in the header file.
process_comparator(void *A, void *B):
Compare the priorities of two processes similar to the HWK2 compare_ints().
If process A has higher priority(lower priority value) than process B, return negative integer.
If process A has lower priority(higher priority value) than process B, return positive integer.
If both process A and process B have equal priority, then decide the priority based on the arrival time of both the processes. If process A arrived first, process A should be considered higher priority. Handle the process_comparator() accordingly.
simulate_preemptive_priority_scheduling(Process processes[], int n, int snapshot_times[], int snapshot_count):
Receives all the process details and snapshot times in its argument.
Consider we have two CPUs(CPU0 and CPU1) and simulate process execution in both the CPUs at each clock cycle using the below logic:
Define a local variable to track the current clock cycle/timestamp(represented by current_time).
Check for processes whose arrival_time == current_time and insert them into the priority queue.
The priority queue must always be ordered by highest priority first (i.e., lowest priority number).
Pick the two highest priority processes from the queue and assign them to CPU0 and CPU1 respectively.
If only one process is available in the queue, assign the available process to CPU0 and the CPU1 will be marked IDLE.
If no process is available, mark both the CPUs as IDLE in its gantt chart.
Run each selected process for 1 clock cycle.
Decrease the remaining_time of the process by 1.
If a process completes execution(remaining_time == 0), then that process won't be available in the priority queue anymore. It would be fully removed.
In each iteration you need to make sure the priority_queue is properly reordered whenever there is an insertion or deletion to the priority queue.
For each CPU, maintain a Gantt chart that records the process being executed at each time unit.
At the end of each clock cycle, check the snapshot_times[] and if the current_time matches any of the snapshot times, print the current state of the priority queue and Gantt chart.
Continue this simulation until all processes have completed execution.
At the end of the simulation(i.e, all the process are executed fully), print the two completed Gantt charts.
Updating Gantt Chart:
Gantt chart is an array of strings: char gantt_chart_cpu0[MAX_TIME][10]. Where MAX_TIME is the maximum clock cycles for simulation to run. We are considering MAX_TIME to be 1000.
Each element in the chart contains a string representing the process that got executed. Represented as PX (where X is the Process ID) or IDLE message.
Tip: use snprintf() to update the gantt chart.
Example gantt chart:
   gantt_chart_cpu0[0] = P1
   gantt_chart_cpu0[1] = P1
   gantt_chart_cpu0[2] = IDLE
print_ready_queue(): print the current priority queue in array representation. Utilize the function from priority_queue.c to implement this.
print_gantt_chart(): Generates a Gantt chart that visually represents the scheduling timeline.
Each chart should display the timeline of processes(or IDLE) at each time unit, along with the time unit.
You can find the reference in the test.out file.

Note: consider the time stamp to start from 0.


3.3 Output:

Snapshot of queue and gantt charts(CPU0 and CPU1) for all the timestamps passed in snapshot_times[].
Final gantt charts(CPU0 and CPU1).
Please use the test.out as a reference on how to print the gantt charts and the queue. Try to format the output to look similar to the reference from test.out.

3.4 Files for the program:

The following files are provided and must be submitted:
Makefile: Do not modify this file. Running make should produce an executable named prog2.
priority_queue.h: Contains declarations of all priority queue-related functions.
priority_queue.c: Implementation of all declared functions.
preemptive_scheduling.h: Contains declarations for all CPU scheduling functions required.
preemptive_scheduling.c: Contains the implementation of the preemptive priority scheduling algorithm.
test.c: Contains test cases with main(). You may add more test cases.
test.out: Contains the expected output for test.c.

name: MazeGame
description: It is an event for SCAI

docs.content:
"""
Advanced Maze-Solving Algorithm with Strategic Powerup Collection

This algorithm uses an enhanced A* search that:
1. Efficiently finds the shortest path to the goal
2. Strategically collects high-value powerups along the way
3. Explores the maze when the goal isn't visible
4. Handles different powerup types with weighted values
"""

from collections import deque
import heapq
import math

def solve_maze(maze, start_pos, goal_pos):
    """
    Solves the maze with intelligent powerup collection.
    
    Args:
        maze: 2D numeric matrix where:
              0 = path, 1 = wall, 2 = start, 3 = goal
              4-11 = various powerups (e.g., 4 = Speed, 5 = Teleport, etc.)
        start_pos: Tuple (x, y) of starting position
        goal_pos: Tuple (x, y) of goal position
        
    Returns:
        List of coordinates [(x1,y1), (x2,y2), ...] from start toward goal
    """
    # Get maze dimensions
    height = len(maze)
    width = len(maze[0]) if height > 0 else 0
    
    # Get information about powerups in the maze
    powerups = find_all_powerups(maze, width, height)
    
    # Check if goal is directly visible and reachable
    goal_visible = is_position_valid(maze, goal_pos)
    
    # If goal is visible and powerups exist, use the strategic A* approach
    if goal_visible and powerups:
        print("Goal is visible and powerups exist - using strategic A* with powerup collection")
        return strategic_a_star(maze, start_pos, goal_pos, powerups)
    elif goal_visible:
        # Goal is visible but no powerups - use regular A* for optimal pathfinding
        print("Goal is visible but no powerups - using standard A*")
        return a_star_search(maze, start_pos, goal_pos)
    else:
        # Goal not visible - use intelligent exploration
        print("Goal not visible - using exploration mode")
        return explore_maze(maze, start_pos, goal_pos, width, height)

def find_all_powerups(maze, width, height):
    """Find and evaluate all powerups in the maze"""
    powerups = []
    
    # Define the powerup types and their base values (higher = more valuable)
    powerup_values = {
        4: 8,    # Speed - very valuable for reducing move cooldown
        5: 10,   # Teleport - gets you closer to the goal
        6: 12,   # Wall_Break - can create shortcuts
        7: 15,   # Score_Multiplier - highest value for score
        8: 9,    # Time_Freeze - good for preventing score decay
        9: 14,   # Ghost - very valuable for passing through walls
        10: 7,   # Decay_Freeze - good but less valuable than others
        11: 11   # Objective - good point value
    }
    
    # Find all powerups in the maze
    for y in range(height):
        for x in range(width):
            cell_value = maze[y][x]
            if 4 <= cell_value <= 11:  # This is a powerup
                powerup_type = cell_value
                base_value = powerup_values.get(powerup_type, 0)
                powerups.append((powerup_type, (x, y), base_value))
    
    # Print summary of powerups found
    if powerups:
        print(f"Found {len(powerups)} powerups in the maze:")
        powerup_names = {
            4: "Speed", 5: "Teleport", 6: "Wall_Break", 
            7: "Score_Multiplier", 8: "Time_Freeze", 
            9: "Ghost", 10: "Decay_Freeze", 11: "Objective"
        }
        
        for p_type, _, _ in powerups:
            print(f"- {powerup_names.get(p_type, f'Unknown ({p_type})')}")
    
    return powerups

def strategic_a_star(maze, start_pos, goal_pos, powerups):
    """A* algorithm that strategically collects valuable powerups on the way to the goal"""
    
    def calculate_powerup_value(powerup_pos, current_pos, remaining_path):
        """Calculate the value of a powerup considering detour cost"""
        # Find the powerup in our list to get its base value
        powerup_info = next((p for p in powerups if p[1] == powerup_pos), None)
        if not powerup_info:
            return 0
        
        powerup_type, _, base_value = powerup_info
        
        # Calculate detour cost: how much extra distance to collect this powerup
        if not remaining_path:
            # If no remaining path, just use direct distances
            direct_to_goal = manhattan_distance(current_pos, goal_pos)
            via_powerup = manhattan_distance(current_pos, powerup_pos) + manhattan_distance(powerup_pos, goal_pos)
            detour_cost = via_powerup - direct_to_goal
        else:
            # Find the closest point on the remaining path to the powerup
            closest_path_point = min(remaining_path, key=lambda p: manhattan_distance(p, powerup_pos))
            direct_to_path_point = manhattan_distance(current_pos, closest_path_point)
            via_powerup = manhattan_distance(current_pos, powerup_pos) + manhattan_distance(powerup_pos, closest_path_point)
            detour_cost = via_powerup - direct_to_path_point
        
        # More valuable powerups are worth a bigger detour
        # Ghost (9) and Wall_Break (6) are especially valuable in mazes
        if powerup_type in [9, 6]:  # Ghost or Wall_Break
            detour_tolerance = base_value * 0.8
        else:
            detour_tolerance = base_value * 0.4
            
        # If detour is too costly compared to powerup value, reduce the value
        if detour_cost > detour_tolerance:
            adjusted_value = base_value - (detour_cost - detour_tolerance)
        else:
            # Within tolerance, give full value plus a bonus for being efficient
            adjusted_value = base_value + (detour_tolerance - detour_cost) * 0.3
            
        return max(0, adjusted_value)  # Ensure value isn't negative
    
    # Create a priority queue for A*: (priority, position, path, collected_powerups)
    open_set = [(manhattan_distance(start_pos, goal_pos), 0, start_pos, [start_pos], [])]
    closed_set = {start_pos: 0}  # position -> g_score (cost from start)
    
    # Track which powerups we've already collected
    collected_positions = set()
    
    while open_set:
        # Get the node with the lowest f_score (priority)
        _, g_score, current, path, collected = heapq.heappop(open_set)
        
        # If we've reached the goal, return the path
        if current == goal_pos:
            print(f"Found path to goal collecting {len(collected)} powerups")
            return path
            
        # Check directions (up, right, down, left)
        for dx, dy in [(0, -1), (1, 0), (0, 1), (-1, 0)]:
            next_pos = (current[0] + dx, current[1] + dy)
            
            # Skip if not a valid move
            if not is_position_valid(maze, next_pos):
                continue
                
            # Calculate the cost to reach this new position
            tentative_g = g_score + 1
            
            # Check if this is a better path to this position
            if next_pos in closed_set and tentative_g >= closed_set[next_pos]:
                continue
                
            # Build the new path
            new_path = path + [next_pos]
            new_collected = collected.copy()
            
            # Check if we've collected a powerup
            x, y = next_pos
            if 4 <= maze[y][x] <= 11 and next_pos not in collected_positions:
                new_collected.append(next_pos)
                collected_positions.add(next_pos)
                
            # Calculate remaining path to goal - use A* to get an optimal path estimate
            remaining_path = a_star_search(maze, next_pos, goal_pos)
            
            # Now look for nearby powerups that might be worth collecting
            nearby_powerup_value = 0
            for _, powerup_pos, _ in powerups:
                if powerup_pos not in collected_positions:
                    value = calculate_powerup_value(powerup_pos, next_pos, remaining_path)
                    nearby_powerup_value += value
                    
            # Calculate heuristic: distance to goal plus a bonus for powerup potential
            h_score = manhattan_distance(next_pos, goal_pos)
            
            # A* formula: f = g + h
            # Lower values for promising paths with good powerup potential
            priority = tentative_g + h_score - (nearby_powerup_value * 0.1)
            
            # Add to open set and update closed set
            heapq.heappush(open_set, (priority, tentative_g, next_pos, new_path, new_collected))
            closed_set[next_pos] = tentative_g
                
    # If no path found, return just the start position
    return [start_pos]

def a_star_search(maze, start_pos, goal_pos):
    """Standard A* search algorithm using Manhattan distance heuristic"""
    # Priority queue for A*: (f_score, g_score, position, path)
    open_set = [(manhattan_distance(start_pos, goal_pos), 0, start_pos, [start_pos])]
    closed_set = {start_pos: 0}  # position -> g_score
    
    while open_set:
        # Get the node with the lowest f_score (priority)
        _, g_score, current, path = heapq.heappop(open_set)
        
        # If we've reached the goal, return the path
        if current == goal_pos:
            return path
            
        # Check directions (up, right, down, left)
        for dx, dy in [(0, -1), (1, 0), (0, 1), (-1, 0)]:
            next_pos = (current[0] + dx, current[1] + dy)
            
            # Skip if not a valid move
            if not is_position_valid(maze, next_pos):
                continue
                
            # Calculate the cost to reach this new position
            tentative_g = g_score + 1
            
            # Check if this is a better path to this position
            if next_pos in closed_set and tentative_g >= closed_set[next_pos]:
                continue
                
            # Build the new path
            new_path = path + [next_pos]
            
            # Calculate priority using f = g + h
            f_score = tentative_g + manhattan_distance(next_pos, goal_pos)
            
            # Add to open set and update closed set
            heapq.heappush(open_set, (f_score, tentative_g, next_pos, new_path))
            closed_set[next_pos] = tentative_g
                
    # If no path found, return just the start position
    return [start_pos]

def explore_maze(maze, start_pos, goal_pos, width, height):
    """
    When the goal isn't visible, explore the maze intelligently
    focusing on high-value frontiers and powerups
    """
    # Find frontier cells - boundaries between explored and unexplored areas
    frontiers = find_frontiers(maze, width, height)
    
    # Extract powerup positions
    powerup_positions = [(pos, base_value) for _, pos, base_value in find_all_powerups(maze, width, height)]
    
    if not frontiers:
        # No frontiers found, try to move in any valid direction
        return find_any_valid_move(maze, start_pos)
    
    # Score frontiers based on multiple factors
    scored_frontiers = []
    for frontier_pos in frontiers:
        # Direction score - how aligned is this with the estimated direction to the goal?
        direction_score = direction_toward_goal(start_pos, frontier_pos, goal_pos)
        
        # Distance score - prefer closer frontiers for efficiency
        distance = manhattan_distance(start_pos, frontier_pos)
        distance_score = 10.0 / (distance + 5)  # Non-linear scaling
        
        # Powerup proximity score - frontiers near powerups are more valuable
        powerup_score = 0
        for p_pos, p_value in powerup_positions:
            proximity = p_value / (manhattan_distance(frontier_pos, p_pos) + 1)
            powerup_score += proximity
            
        # Combined score (weighted)
        score = (direction_score * 0.4) + (distance_score * 0.3) + (powerup_score * 0.3)
        scored_frontiers.append((score, frontier_pos))
    
    # Sort frontiers by score (descending)
    scored_frontiers.sort(reverse=True)
    
    # Try to find path to each frontier, starting with highest scored
    for _, frontier_pos in scored_frontiers[:5]:  # Try top 5 frontiers
        path = bfs_path(maze, start_pos, frontier_pos)
        if path and len(path) > 1:  # Ensure we have a real path
            return path
    
    # No path to any frontier found - move in any valid direction
    return find_any_valid_move(maze, start_pos)

def find_frontiers(maze, width, height):
    """Find frontier cells - open cells adjacent to unexplored areas/walls"""
    frontiers = []
    directions = [(0, -1), (1, 0), (0, 1), (-1, 0)]
    
    for y in range(height):
        for x in range(width):
            # Skip walls and powerups
            if maze[y][x] == 1:  # Wall
                continue
                
            # Check if this cell is at a frontier
            frontier_quality = 0
            for dx, dy in directions:
                nx, ny = x + dx, y + dy
                
                # Count edges of the maze as walls
                if not (0 <= nx < width and 0 <= ny < height):
                    frontier_quality += 1
                    continue
                    
                # Count actual walls
                if maze[ny][nx] == 1:
                    frontier_quality += 1
            
            # A good frontier has some walls but isn't completely surrounded
            if 1 <= frontier_quality <= 2:
                frontiers.append((x, y))
    
    return frontiers

def bfs_path(maze, start_pos, goal_pos):
    """Standard BFS to find a path between two points"""
    queue = deque([(start_pos, [start_pos])])
    visited = {start_pos}
    
    while queue:
        current, path = queue.popleft()
        
        if current == goal_pos:
            return path
            
        for dx, dy in [(0, -1), (1, 0), (0, 1), (-1, 0)]:
            next_pos = (current[0] + dx, current[1] + dy)
            
            if is_position_valid(maze, next_pos) and next_pos not in visited:
                visited.add(next_pos)
                queue.append((next_pos, path + [next_pos]))
    
    return None

def find_any_valid_move(maze, start_pos):
    """Find any valid move from the current position"""
    x, y = start_pos
    
    # Check for powerups first
    for dx, dy in [(0, -1), (1, 0), (0, 1), (-1, 0)]:
        nx, ny = x + dx, y + dy
        next_pos = (nx, ny)
        
        if is_position_valid(maze, next_pos):
            # Prioritize powerups
            if 4 <= maze[ny][nx] <= 11:
                return [start_pos, next_pos]
                
    # Then try any valid move
    for dx, dy in [(0, -1), (1, 0), (0, 1), (-1, 0)]:
        nx, ny = x + dx, y + dy
        next_pos = (nx, ny)
        
        if is_position_valid(maze, next_pos):
            return [start_pos, next_pos]
    
    # No valid moves, stay in place
    return [start_pos]

def is_position_valid(maze, pos):
    """Check if a position is valid (in bounds and not a wall)"""
    x, y = pos
    height = len(maze)
    width = len(maze[0]) if height > 0 else 0
    
    if not (0 <= x < width and 0 <= y < height):
        return False
    
    # Any value except 1 (wall) is passable
    return maze[y][x] != 1

def manhattan_distance(pos1, pos2):
    """Calculate Manhattan distance between two positions"""
    return abs(pos1[0] - pos2[0]) + abs(pos1[1] - pos2[1])

def direction_toward_goal(start_pos, frontier_pos, goal_pos):
    """Calculate how well aligned a direction is with the estimated direction to goal"""
    # Vector from start to frontier
    v1_x = frontier_pos[0] - start_pos[0]
    v1_y = frontier_pos[1] - start_pos[1]
    
    # Vector from start to goal
    v2_x = goal_pos[0] - start_pos[0]
    v2_y = goal_pos[1] - start_pos[1]
    
    # Normalize vectors (avoiding division by zero)
    v1_mag = math.sqrt(v1_x**2 + v1_y**2)
    v2_mag = math.sqrt(v2_x**2 + v2_y**2)
    
    if v1_mag == 0 or v2_mag == 0:
        return 0.5  # Neutral score for zero vectors
    
    v1_x, v1_y = v1_x/v1_mag, v1_y/v1_mag
    v2_x, v2_y = v2_x/v2_mag, v2_y/v2_mag
    
    # Dot product gives cosine of angle between vectors (-1 to 1)
    dot_product = v1_x*v2_x + v1_y*v2_y
    
    # Map from [-1, 1] to [0, 1] where 1 means perfectly aligned
    return (dot_product + 1) / 2

"""
Advanced Maze-Solving Algorithm with Strategic Powerup Collection

This algorithm uses an enhanced A* search that:
1. Efficiently finds the shortest path to the goal
2. Strategically collects high-value powerups along the way
3. Explores the maze when the goal isn't visible
4. Handles different powerup types with weighted values
"""

from collections import deque
import heapq
import math

def solve_maze(maze, start_pos, goal_pos):
    """
    Solves the maze with intelligent powerup collection.
    
    Args:
        maze: 2D numeric matrix where:
              0 = path, 1 = wall, 2 = start, 3 = goal
              4-11 = various powerups (e.g., 4 = Speed, 5 = Teleport, etc.)
        start_pos: Tuple (x, y) of starting position
        goal_pos: Tuple (x, y) of goal position
        
    Returns:
        List of coordinates [(x1,y1), (x2,y2), ...] from start toward goal
    """
    # Get maze dimensions
    height = len(maze)
    width = len(maze[0]) if height > 0 else 0
    
    # Get information about powerups in the maze
    powerups = find_all_powerups(maze, width, height)
    
    # Check if goal is directly visible and reachable
    goal_visible = is_position_valid(maze, goal_pos)
    
    # If goal is visible and powerups exist, use the strategic A* approach
    if goal_visible and powerups:
        print("Goal is visible and powerups exist - using strategic A* with powerup collection")
        return strategic_a_star(maze, start_pos, goal_pos, powerups)
    elif goal_visible:
        # Goal is visible but no powerups - use regular A* for optimal pathfinding
        print("Goal is visible but no powerups - using standard A*")
        return a_star_search(maze, start_pos, goal_pos)
    else:
        # Goal not visible - use intelligent exploration
        print("Goal not visible - using exploration mode")
        return explore_maze(maze, start_pos, goal_pos, width, height)

def find_all_powerups(maze, width, height):
    """Find and evaluate all powerups in the maze"""
    powerups = []
    
    # Define the powerup types and their base values (higher = more valuable)
    powerup_values = {
        4: 8,    # Speed - very valuable for reducing move cooldown
        5: 10,   # Teleport - gets you closer to the goal
        6: 12,   # Wall_Break - can create shortcuts
        7: 15,   # Score_Multiplier - highest value for score
        8: 9,    # Time_Freeze - good for preventing score decay
        9: 14,   # Ghost - very valuable for passing through walls
        10: 7,   # Decay_Freeze - good but less valuable than others
        11: 11   # Objective - good point value
    }
    
    # Find all powerups in the maze
    for y in range(height):
        for x in range(width):
            cell_value = maze[y][x]
            if 4 <= cell_value <= 11:  # This is a powerup
                powerup_type = cell_value
                base_value = powerup_values.get(powerup_type, 0)
                powerups.append((powerup_type, (x, y), base_value))
    
    # Print summary of powerups found
    if powerups:
        print(f"Found {len(powerups)} powerups in the maze:")
        powerup_names = {
            4: "Speed", 5: "Teleport", 6: "Wall_Break", 
            7: "Score_Multiplier", 8: "Time_Freeze", 
            9: "Ghost", 10: "Decay_Freeze", 11: "Objective"
        }
        
        for p_type, _, _ in powerups:
            print(f"- {powerup_names.get(p_type, f'Unknown ({p_type})')}")
    
    return powerups

def strategic_a_star(maze, start_pos, goal_pos, powerups):
    """A* algorithm that strategically collects valuable powerups on the way to the goal"""
    
    def calculate_powerup_value(powerup_pos, current_pos, remaining_path):
        """Calculate the value of a powerup considering detour cost"""
        # Find the powerup in our list to get its base value
        powerup_info = next((p for p in powerups if p[1] == powerup_pos), None)
        if not powerup_info:
            return 0
        
        powerup_type, _, base_value = powerup_info
        
        # Calculate detour cost: how much extra distance to collect this powerup
        if not remaining_path:
            # If no remaining path, just use direct distances
            direct_to_goal = manhattan_distance(current_pos, goal_pos)
            via_powerup = manhattan_distance(current_pos, powerup_pos) + manhattan_distance(powerup_pos, goal_pos)
            detour_cost = via_powerup - direct_to_goal
        else:
            # Find the closest point on the remaining path to the powerup
            closest_path_point = min(remaining_path, key=lambda p: manhattan_distance(p, powerup_pos))
            direct_to_path_point = manhattan_distance(current_pos, closest_path_point)
            via_powerup = manhattan_distance(current_pos, powerup_pos) + manhattan_distance(powerup_pos, closest_path_point)
            detour_cost = via_powerup - direct_to_path_point
        
        # More valuable powerups are worth a bigger detour
        # Ghost (9) and Wall_Break (6) are especially valuable in mazes
        if powerup_type in [9, 6]:  # Ghost or Wall_Break
            detour_tolerance = base_value * 0.8
        else:
            detour_tolerance = base_value * 0.4
            
        # If detour is too costly compared to powerup value, reduce the value
        if detour_cost > detour_tolerance:
            adjusted_value = base_value - (detour_cost - detour_tolerance)
        else:
            # Within tolerance, give full value plus a bonus for being efficient
            adjusted_value = base_value + (detour_tolerance - detour_cost) * 0.3
            
        return max(0, adjusted_value)  # Ensure value isn't negative
    
    # Create a priority queue for A*: (priority, position, path, collected_powerups)
    open_set = [(manhattan_distance(start_pos, goal_pos), 0, start_pos, [start_pos], [])]
    closed_set = {start_pos: 0}  # position -> g_score (cost from start)
    
    # Track which powerups we've already collected
    collected_positions = set()
    
    while open_set:
        # Get the node with the lowest f_score (priority)
        _, g_score, current, path, collected = heapq.heappop(open_set)
        
        # If we've reached the goal, return the path
        if current == goal_pos:
            print(f"Found path to goal collecting {len(collected)} powerups")
            return path
            
        # Check directions (up, right, down, left)
        for dx, dy in [(0, -1), (1, 0), (0, 1), (-1, 0)]:
            next_pos = (current[0] + dx, current[1] + dy)
            
            # Skip if not a valid move
            if not is_position_valid(maze, next_pos):
                continue
                
            # Calculate the cost to reach this new position
            tentative_g = g_score + 1
            
            # Check if this is a better path to this position
            if next_pos in closed_set and tentative_g >= closed_set[next_pos]:
                continue
                
            # Build the new path
            new_path = path + [next_pos]
            new_collected = collected.copy()
            
            # Check if we've collected a powerup
            x, y = next_pos
            if 4 <= maze[y][x] <= 11 and next_pos not in collected_positions:
                new_collected.append(next_pos)
                collected_positions.add(next_pos)
                
            # Calculate remaining path to goal - use A* to get an optimal path estimate
            remaining_path = a_star_search(maze, next_pos, goal_pos)
            
            # Now look for nearby powerups that might be worth collecting
            nearby_powerup_value = 0
            for _, powerup_pos, _ in powerups:
                if powerup_pos not in collected_positions:
                    value = calculate_powerup_value(powerup_pos, next_pos, remaining_path)
                    nearby_powerup_value += value
                    
            # Calculate heuristic: distance to goal plus a bonus for powerup potential
            h_score = manhattan_distance(next_pos, goal_pos)
            
            # A* formula: f = g + h
            # Lower values for promising paths with good powerup potential
            priority = tentative_g + h_score - (nearby_powerup_value * 0.1)
            
            # Add to open set and update closed set
            heapq.heappush(open_set, (priority, tentative_g, next_pos, new_path, new_collected))
            closed_set[next_pos] = tentative_g
                
    # If no path found, return just the start position
    return [start_pos]

def a_star_search(maze, start_pos, goal_pos):
    """Standard A* search algorithm using Manhattan distance heuristic"""
    # Priority queue for A*: (f_score, g_score, position, path)
    open_set = [(manhattan_distance(start_pos, goal_pos), 0, start_pos, [start_pos])]
    closed_set = {start_pos: 0}  # position -> g_score
    
    while open_set:
        # Get the node with the lowest f_score (priority)
        _, g_score, current, path = heapq.heappop(open_set)
        
        # If we've reached the goal, return the path
        if current == goal_pos:
            return path
            
        # Check directions (up, right, down, left)
        for dx, dy in [(0, -1), (1, 0), (0, 1), (-1, 0)]:
            next_pos = (current[0] + dx, current[1] + dy)
            
            # Skip if not a valid move
            if not is_position_valid(maze, next_pos):
                continue
                
            # Calculate the cost to reach this new position
            tentative_g = g_score + 1
            
            # Check if this is a better path to this position
            if next_pos in closed_set and tentative_g >= closed_set[next_pos]:
                continue
                
            # Build the new path
            new_path = path + [next_pos]
            
            # Calculate priority using f = g + h
            f_score = tentative_g + manhattan_distance(next_pos, goal_pos)
            
            # Add to open set and update closed set
            heapq.heappush(open_set, (f_score, tentative_g, next_pos, new_path))
            closed_set[next_pos] = tentative_g
                
    # If no path found, return just the start position
    return [start_pos]

def explore_maze(maze, start_pos, goal_pos, width, height):
    """
    When the goal isn't visible, explore the maze intelligently
    focusing on high-value frontiers and powerups
    """
    # Find frontier cells - boundaries between explored and unexplored areas
    frontiers = find_frontiers(maze, width, height)
    
    # Extract powerup positions
    powerup_positions = [(pos, base_value) for _, pos, base_value in find_all_powerups(maze, width, height)]
    
    if not frontiers:
        # No frontiers found, try to move in any valid direction
        return find_any_valid_move(maze, start_pos)
    
    # Score frontiers based on multiple factors
    scored_frontiers = []
    for frontier_pos in frontiers:
        # Direction score - how aligned is this with the estimated direction to the goal?
        direction_score = direction_toward_goal(start_pos, frontier_pos, goal_pos)
        
        # Distance score - prefer closer frontiers for efficiency
        distance = manhattan_distance(start_pos, frontier_pos)
        distance_score = 10.0 / (distance + 5)  # Non-linear scaling
        
        # Powerup proximity score - frontiers near powerups are more valuable
        powerup_score = 0
        for p_pos, p_value in powerup_positions:
            proximity = p_value / (manhattan_distance(frontier_pos, p_pos) + 1)
            powerup_score += proximity
            
        # Combined score (weighted)
        score = (direction_score * 0.4) + (distance_score * 0.3) + (powerup_score * 0.3)
        scored_frontiers.append((score, frontier_pos))
    
    # Sort frontiers by score (descending)
    scored_frontiers.sort(reverse=True)
    
    # Try to find path to each frontier, starting with highest scored
    for _, frontier_pos in scored_frontiers[:5]:  # Try top 5 frontiers
        path = bfs_path(maze, start_pos, frontier_pos)
        if path and len(path) > 1:  # Ensure we have a real path
            return path
    
    # No path to any frontier found - move in any valid direction
    return find_any_valid_move(maze, start_pos)

def find_frontiers(maze, width, height):
    """Find frontier cells - open cells adjacent to unexplored areas/walls"""
    frontiers = []
    directions = [(0, -1), (1, 0), (0, 1), (-1, 0)]
    
    for y in range(height):
        for x in range(width):
            # Skip walls and powerups
            if maze[y][x] == 1:  # Wall
                continue
                
            # Check if this cell is at a frontier
            frontier_quality = 0
            for dx, dy in directions:
                nx, ny = x + dx, y + dy
                
                # Count edges of the maze as walls
                if not (0 <= nx < width and 0 <= ny < height):
                    frontier_quality += 1
                    continue
                    
                # Count actual walls
                if maze[ny][nx] == 1:
                    frontier_quality += 1
            
            # A good frontier has some walls but isn't completely surrounded
            if 1 <= frontier_quality <= 2:
                frontiers.append((x, y))
    
    return frontiers

def bfs_path(maze, start_pos, goal_pos):
    """Standard BFS to find a path between two points"""
    queue = deque([(start_pos, [start_pos])])
    visited = {start_pos}
    
    while queue:
        current, path = queue.popleft()
        
        if current == goal_pos:
            return path
            
        for dx, dy in [(0, -1), (1, 0), (0, 1), (-1, 0)]:
            next_pos = (current[0] + dx, current[1] + dy)
            
            if is_position_valid(maze, next_pos) and next_pos not in visited:
                visited.add(next_pos)
                queue.append((next_pos, path + [next_pos]))
    
    return None

def find_any_valid_move(maze, start_pos):
    """Find any valid move from the current position"""
    x, y = start_pos
    
    # Check for powerups first
    for dx, dy in [(0, -1), (1, 0), (0, 1), (-1, 0)]:
        nx, ny = x + dx, y + dy
        next_pos = (nx, ny)
        
        if is_position_valid(maze, next_pos):
            # Prioritize powerups
            if 4 <= maze[ny][nx] <= 11:
                return [start_pos, next_pos]
                
    # Then try any valid move
    for dx, dy in [(0, -1), (1, 0), (0, 1), (-1, 0)]:
        nx, ny = x + dx, y + dy
        next_pos = (nx, ny)
        
        if is_position_valid(maze, next_pos):
            return [start_pos, next_pos]
    
    # No valid moves, stay in place
    return [start_pos]

def is_position_valid(maze, pos):
    """Check if a position is valid (in bounds and not a wall)"""
    x, y = pos
    height = len(maze)
    width = len(maze[0]) if height > 0 else 0
    
    if not (0 <= x < width and 0 <= y < height):
        return False
    
    # Any value except 1 (wall) is passable
    return maze[y][x] != 1

def manhattan_distance(pos1, pos2):
    """Calculate Manhattan distance between two positions"""
    return abs(pos1[0] - pos2[0]) + abs(pos1[1] - pos2[1])

def direction_toward_goal(start_pos, frontier_pos, goal_pos):
    """Calculate how well aligned a direction is with the estimated direction to goal"""
    # Vector from start to frontier
    v1_x = frontier_pos[0] - start_pos[0]
    v1_y = frontier_pos[1] - start_pos[1]
    
    # Vector from start to goal
    v2_x = goal_pos[0] - start_pos[0]
    v2_y = goal_pos[1] - start_pos[1]
    
    # Normalize vectors (avoiding division by zero)
    v1_mag = math.sqrt(v1_x**2 + v1_y**2)
    v2_mag = math.sqrt(v2_x**2 + v2_y**2)
    
    if v1_mag == 0 or v2_mag == 0:
        return 0.5  # Neutral score for zero vectors
    
    v1_x, v1_y = v1_x/v1_mag, v1_y/v1_mag
    v2_x, v2_y = v2_x/v2_mag, v2_y/v2_mag
    
    # Dot product gives cosine of angle between vectors (-1 to 1)
    dot_product = v1_x*v2_x + v1_y*v2_y
    
    # Map from [-1, 1] to [0, 1] where 1 means perfectly aligned
    return (dot_product + 1) / 2

Changelog:
1. Objectives:
Learn how to implement dictionary and hash table data structures.
Build mappings between words and unique numerical IDs.
Understand the basics of how modern AI systems like chatbots process text.
Prepare the foundation for a later program that builds a simplified Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) system.
2. Background:
Modern Large language Models (like ChatGPT) don't work directly with full words or sentences - they break input text into smaller pieces called tokens. For example:
The sentence The cat sat. might become tokens: ["The", "cat", "sat", "."].
Or even: ["Th", "e", "cat", "sat", "."] depending on how the system is designed.
Each token is then mapped to a unique number (ID) because computers work efficiently with numbers, not raw text.
This process of turning text into tokens is called tokenization. The collection of all tokens the system knows is called its vocabulary. And the mappings between token strings and IDs are stored in fast lookup structures like dictionaries and hash tables.

Even though this homework does not ask you to implement full tokenization algorithms, you will build:

a dictionary that stores token → ID pairs,
a hash table that speeds up the lookups,
and a simple program that reads a small text corpus and assigns unique IDs to words.
This will prepare you for the next stage, where you'll build the more advanced BPE (Byte Pair Encoding) tokenizer used in modern large language models (LLMs).
3. Description:
In this assignment, you will:
Complete the missing functions in Dictionary.c to create and manage a dictionary.
Implement the core hash function ht_hash in HashTable.c.
Use the provided hwk3.c to build a word-level vocabulary (no subword splitting) from a given text corpus. The provided hwk3.c program serves as a word-level vocabulary builder and tokenizer. It reads a text corpus, assigns each unique word a unique numerical ID (the same id is applied to multiple occurrences of the same word, which means each unique key maps to exactly one unique value), and then allows users to input new sentences to see how they are tokenized into ID sequences. This warmup gives you a hands-on understanding of how token → ID mappings work - a crucial foundation before you implement the more advanced Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) system in the next part of the lab.
Test your implementation by inputting new sentences and seeing how they get mapped to token IDs.
4. Step-by-Step Tasks:
Step 1: Implement Dictionary Functions (Dictionary.c)

You need to implement the following functions (see Dictionary.h for full specs):
dictionary_create: Create a new dictionary with a given number of slots and a printer function.
dictionary_destroy: Free all memory used by the dictionary.
dictionary_insert: Insert a KVPair (key-value pair) into the dictionary. Return false if the key already exists. There is no need to handle the situation that the dictionary is full, which will not be included in our test cases.
dictionary_delete: Remove a KVPair from the dictionary by key; return the removed pair or NULL if not found.
dictionary_find: Look up a KVPair by key; return the found pair or NULL if not found.
dictionary_print: Print the entire dictionary in the format key: value.
These functions use a hash table internally, where each slot contains a linked list of KVPair items.
Step 2: Implement Hash Function (HashTable.c)

You only need to complete:
ht_hash: Given a string key and the number of slots, compute the correct index in the hash table.
You will use the provided helper:
ht_string2int: Converts a string to an integer hash value. This function is given to ensure the same store order of vocabulary.
Step 3: Test Using Provided Program (hwk3.c)

Once you've completed the above:
Compile the provided hwk3.c program. (make)
Run it with: ./hwk3 corpus.txt < test.in
The program will:
Read the corpus (corpus.txt) and build a word-level vocabulary (no BPE yet).
Assign a unique ID (starting from 0) to each word.
Store:
word → ID (in the token_to_id dictionary).
ID → word (in the id_to_token dictionary).
Print the vocabulary.
Prompt you to enter new sentences, and return the token ID sequence.
5. Example input and output
Given this corpus.txt and test.in. The program outputs test.out.
corpus.txt:

      
      transformer attention mechanism
      transformer model
      embedding layer
      attention mechanism
      transformer attention embedding
      
    
Vocabulary (the order is decided by hash function, which has been provided in HashTable.c):
      
      transformer: 0
      mechanism: 2
      model: 3
      attention: 1
      embedding: 4
      layer: 5        
      
    
Encode input sentences (test.in) according to the vocabulary:
      
      transformer model attention mechanism: 0 3 1 2 
      embedding attention transformer: 4 1 0

Changelog:
1. Objectives:
Understand the Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) algorithm, one of the most widely used tokenization methods in modern large language models (LLMs).
Implement a character-level BPE tokenizer that builds a compact subword vocabulary (Note: this lab is a simplified version of the BPE algorithm used in real-world AI applications, so it may differ in some ways. Please follow the lab spec and complete the lab as instructed.)
Use the Dictionary.c and HashTable.c implementations you completed in the hwk3 to store token-to-ID mappings.
Build an end-to-end tokenizer that can:
Train BPE merges from a text corpus.
Create a subword vocabulary.
Tokenize new input sentences using a greedy longest-match strategy.
2. Background:
Modern LLMs (like GPT, BERT, or LLaMA) need to handle huge vocabularies efficiently. Instead of splitting text into just words, they use subword units to capture patterns like:
Common roots (play, player, played → play + er / ed)
Rare words broken into parts (encyclopedia → encyclo + pedia)
Even single characters when needed.
One of the most popular methods to build such subword vocabularies is Byte Pair Encoding (BPE):
Start with character-level tokens (including a special end-of-word marker like </w>).
Iteratively merge the most frequent adjacent token pairs in the corpus (e.g., merge t + h into th if it appears most often).
Repeat until you reach a desired vocabulary size or a set number of merge operations.
Use the resulting merged tokens for compact and flexible tokenization.
This approach balances compression (fewer tokens) and flexibility (can handle rare or unseen words by falling back to smaller units).
Example for simplified BPE (used in this lab):


    corpus.txt:
      low lower newest widest
    
    Initial tokenization: 
      low: l o w </w>
      Lower: l o w e r </w>
      Newest: n e w e s t </w>
      Widest: w i d e s t </w>
    
    
    Iteration 0 (initialize the vocabulary): [l, o, w, </w>, e, r, n, s, t, i, d]

    Iteration 1: 
      pair frequency:
        (l, o): 2
        (o, w): 2
        (w, </w>): 1
        (w, e): 1
        (e, r): 1
        (n, e): 1
        (e, w): 1
        (w, e): 1
        (e, s): 2
        (s, t): 2
        (w, i): 1
        (i, d): 1
        (d, e): 1
      Choose:
        (l, o) → max_frequency (if same frequency, choose the first one, left to right in corpus)
    
    Tokenization after merge:
      lo w </w>
      lo w e r </w>
      n e w e s t </w>
      w i d e s t </w>
    
    Iteration 2: 
      pair frequency:
        (lo, w): 2
        (w, </w>): 1
        (w, e): 1
        (e, r): 1
        (n, e): 1
        (e, w): 1
        (w, e): 1
        (e, s): 2
        (s, t): 2
        (w, i): 1
        (i, d): 1
        (d, e): 1
    
      Choose:
        (lo, w)
    
    Tokenization after merge:
      low </w>
      low e r </w>
      n e w e s t </w>
      w i d e s t </w>
    
    Iteration 3: 
      ...
    
3. Description:
In this assignment, you will:
Implement the full BPE training and tokenization pipeline (in bpe.c).
Reuse the Dictionary.c and HashTable.c implementations you completed earlier.
Complete bpe.c program (4 functions to fulfill).
4. Step-by-Step Tasks:
Step 1: Read Corpus as Character Sequences

Read the input corpus file (provided as a command-line argument).
For each word:
Split it into individual characters.
Add a special end-of-word token </w> to mark word boundaries. Note that we treat </w> as 1 token (similar to 1 character, cannot be further divided).
e.g. low → l o w </w>
Store these character sequences (each word is split into one character sequence, use the Sentence structure to store) in a data structure (Sentence array) for later processing.
Step 2: Run BPE Training

For a fixed number of iterations (For this assignment, 10 iterations):
Scan the corpus to count frequencies of all adjacent token pairs.
Identify the most frequent pair. (if same frequency, choose the first one with most frequency)
Merge this pair into a new combined token (e.g., merge t + h → th).
Update the corpus (update Sentence array) to reflect this merged token.
Repeat until either:
You complete the max number of iterations.
Or no more pairs are found to merge.
Step 3: Build the Final Vocabulary

After BPE training (step2), collect all unique tokens present in the corpus (traverse Sentence array).
Add each token to your token_to_id dictionary, assigning unique IDs (IDs start from 0 and increment by 1, assign from left to right). This is the BPE vocabulary for the input corpus.
Step 4: Implement the BPE Tokenizer

When a new sentence is provided:
For each word:
Append </w> to the word.
Greedily match the longest possible token from the vocabulary, scanning left to right within the word.
At each position, try to match the longest prefix of the remaining string that is present in the vocabulary.
Once a match is found, emit that token and continue matching from the next position after that token. Print both the matched token and its ID. i.e. Word 'newest': [ne -> 2] [w -> 3] [est</w> -> 4]
If no match is found at a position, emit [UNK(character)] and move one character forward. i.e. Word 'xxlow': [UNK(x)] [UNK(x)] [low</w> -> 0]
This greedy matching ensures the tokenizer uses the largest known subword units and breaks ties by choosing the leftmost match first.
Greedy Matching Example:

      Suppose your vocabulary contains the following tokens:
          l, lo, low, e, es, est, w, s, t, </w>
      Now consider the word lowest (which becomes lowest</w> internally):
        - At position 0: The tokenizer checks all prefixes starting from position 0 and finds that low is the longest match (instead of l or lo).
          → Match: [low]
        - Continue at position 3: The next character is e. From this position, the longest match is est.
          → Match: [est]
        - Continue at position 6: The remaining token is </w>.
          → Match: [</w>]
      Final tokenization: [low] [est] [</w>]
      This example demonstrates how the tokenizer always matches the longest possible token starting from the current position, then moves forward to repeat the process.
  
Note: All students should produce the same tokenization output, as well as the same vocabulary is built from the same corpus. The greedy matching strategy is deterministic.

Tip: Refer to the provided examples and test cases to help verify correctness.

Step 5: Run your code.

Once you've completed the above:
Compile the bpe.c program using the provided Makefile: make
Run it with: ./prog3 corpus.txt < test.in
The program will:
Read the corpus file (corpus.txt)
Each line is split into words.
Each word is broken into individual characters plus an </w> end marker.
The words are stored as token sequences inside the corpus.
Train Byte Pair Encoding (BPE):
The program will print each merge operation
The vocabulary is built iteratively by merging the most frequent adjacent token pairs.

      Iteration 1: merging 'l' + 'o'
      Iteration 2: merging 'lo' + 'w'
      ...
    
Display the learned vocabulary:
After training, it prints the token-to-ID mapping in this format:
Interactive tokenization:
The program waits for user input.
Each input sentence is tokenized using the learned BPE rules (greedy matching).
It prints for each word:
Which subwords matched
Their corresponding token IDs

      Word 'low': [low</w> -> 0] 
      Word 'lower': [lower</w> -> 1] 
      ...
    
5. Example input and output
Given this corpus.txt and test.in. The program outputs test.out.
Note: If your corpus.txt and test.in inputs are exactly the same as the provided example, your program's output - including the BPE training process (merge steps), the vocabulary token-to-ID mapping, the order of vocabulary output, and the tokenization of input sentences - should be identical to the example output. If you see any discrepancies, please double-check the lab spec and your code logic to ensure that your implementation follows the deterministic merge strategy and greedy matching rules.

Your output should be able to pass "diff" test.

corpus.txt:

      
        low lower newest widest large
      
    
BPE train process:
      
        Iteration 1: merging 'l' + 'o'
        Iteration 2: merging 'lo' + 'w'
        Iteration 3: merging 'e' + 's'
        Iteration 4: merging 'es' + 't'
        Iteration 5: merging 'est' + '</w>'
        Iteration 6: merging 'low' + '</w>'
        Iteration 7: merging 'low' + 'e'
        Iteration 8: merging 'lowe' + 'r'
        Iteration 9: merging 'lower' + '</w>'
        Iteration 10: merging 'n' + 'e'         
      
    
Vocabulary (the order is decided by hash function, which has been provided in HashTable.c):
      
        a: 8
        d: 6
        e: 11
        </w>: 12
        lower</w>: 1
        g: 10
        i: 5
        l: 7
        low</w>: 0
        r: 9
        w: 3

Tokenize input sentences (test.in) according to the vocabulary:
      
        Word 'low': [low</w> -> 0]

        Word 'newest': [ne -> 2] [w -> 3] [est</w> -> 4] 
        Word 'widest': [w -> 3] [i -> 5] [d -> 6] [est</w> -> 4]

6. Files Provided

name: Philosophy lecture
description: generating  questions to ask the class because I cannot be fucked

docs.content:
1
Testimonial Injustice
In Anthony Minghella’s screenplay of The Talented Mr Ripley, Herbert
Greenleaf uses a familiar put-down to silence Marge Sherwood, the
young woman who, but for the sinister disappearance of his son, Dickie,
was soon to have become his daughter-in-law: ‘Marge, there’s female
intuition, and then there are facts.’¹ Greenleaf is responding to Marge’s
expressed suspicion that Tom Ripley—a supposed friend of Dickie and
Marge, who has curried much favour with Greenleaf senior—is in fact
Dickie’s murderer. It is easy to see that Greenleaf’s silencing of Marge
here involves an exercise of power, and of gender power in particular.
But what do we mean by power? And how does gender power relate
to the general notion of social power? In order to paint a portrait of
testimonial injustice and to home in on its distinctive central case, we
need to answer these questions about the nature of social power in
general and the particular kind of social power (of which gender power
is one instance) that I shall call identity power.
1 . 1 P OW E R
Let us begin from what I take to be the strongly intuitive idea that social
power is a capacity we have as social agents to influence how things go in
the social world. A first point to make is that power can operate actively
or passively. Consider, for example, the power that a traffic warden has
over drivers, which consists in the fact that she can fine them for a
parking offence. Sometimes this power operates actively, as it does when
she actually imposes a fine. But it is crucial that it also operates passively,
as it does whenever her ability to impose such a fine influences a person’s
parking behaviour. There is a relation of dependence between active and
¹ Anthony Minghella, The Talented Mr Ripley—Based on Patricia Highsmith’s Novel
(London: Methuen, 2000), 130.

Testimonial Injustice 23
of prejudices obviously relevant to the context of credibility judgement,
such as the idea that women are irrational, blacks are intellectual-
ly inferior to whites, the working classes are the moral inferiors of
the upper classes, Jews are wily, Orientals are sly ... and so on in a
grim catalogue of clichés more or less likely to insinuate themselves into
judgements of credibility at different moments in history. But in order
to furnish the philosophical imagination less crudely, let us turn to
an example from literature that provides us with a historically truthful
fiction.
The example is from Harper Lee’s To Kill a Mockingbird. The year
is 1935, and the scene a courtroom in Maycomb County, Alabama.
The defendant is a young black man named Tom Robinson. He is
charged with raping a white girl, Mayella Ewell, whose family’s run-
down house he passes every day on his way to work, situated as it is on
the outskirts of town in the borderlands that divide where whites and
blacks live. It is obvious to the reader, and to any relatively unprejudiced
person in the courtroom, that Tom Robinson is entirely innocent.
For Atticus Finch, our politely spoken counsel for the defence, has
proved beyond doubt that Robinson could not have beaten the Ewell
girl so as to cause the sort of cuts and bruises she sustained that day,
since whoever gave her the beating led with his left fist, whereas Tom
Robinson’s left arm is disabled, having been injured in a machinery
accident when he was a boy. The trial proceedings enact what is in one
sense a straightforward struggle between the power of evidence and the
power of racial prejudice, with the all-white jury’s judgement ultimately
succumbing to the latter. But the psychology is subtle, and there is a
great complexity of social meanings at work in determining the jury’s
perception of Tom Robinson as a speaker. In a showdown between the
word of a black man and that of a poor white girl, the courtroom air
is thick with the ‘do’s and ‘don’t’s of racial politics. Telling the truth
here is a minefield for Tom Robinson, since if he casts aspersions on
the white girl, he will be perceived as a presumptuous, lying Negro; yet,
if he does not publicize Mayella Ewell’s attempt to kiss him (which is
what really happened), then a guilty verdict is even more nearly assured.
This discursive predicament mirrors his practical predicament at the
Ewell’s house on that fateful day when Mayella grabbed him. If he
pushes her away, then he will be found to have assaulted her; yet if he
is passive, he will equally be found to have assaulted her. So he does
the most neutral thing he can, which is to run, though knowing all the
while that this action too will be taken as a sign of guilt. Mr Gilmer’s
24 Testimonial Injustice
interrogation of Tom is suffused with the idea that his running away
implies culpability:
‘ ... why did you run so fast?’
‘I says I was scared, suh.’
‘If you had a clear conscience, why were you scared?’¹⁶
Running away, it seems, is something a black man in Maycomb County
cannot do without incriminating himself. Similarly, there are many
things he cannot say in court and stand a chance of being heard as
truthful. At a pivotal moment during the prosecution’s interrogation,
for instance, Tom Robinson makes the mistake of being honest about
his kindly motivations for stopping off at Mayella Ewell’s house as
regularly as he did to help her out with odd jobs. The scene, like the
whole story, is reported from the point of view of Scout, Atticus Finch’s
young daughter, who is secretly surveying the proceedings with her
brother, Jem, from the ‘Negro gallery’. Mr Gilmer, the prosecutor, sets
him up:
‘Why were you so anxious to do that woman’s chores?’
Tom Robinson hesitated, searching for an answer. ‘Looked like she didn’t
have nobody to help her, like I says—’
... Mr Gilmer smiled grimly at the jury. ‘You’re a mighty good fellow, it
seems—did all this for not one penny?’
‘Yes suh. I felt right sorry for her, she seemed to try more’n the rest of ‘em—’
‘You felt sorry for her, you felt sorry for her?’ Mr Gilmer seemed ready to rise
to the ceiling.
The witness realized his mistake and shifted uncomfortably in the chair. But
the damage was done. Below us, nobody liked Tom Robinson’s answer. Mr
Gilmer paused a long time to let it sink in.¹⁷
Here the ‘damage’ in question is done to any epistemic trust that
the white jury has so far been human enough to feel towards the black
testifier. For feeling sorry for someone is a taboo sentiment if you are
black and the object of your sympathy is a white person. In the context
of a racist ideology structured around dogmas of white superiority,
the fundamental ethical sentiment of plain human sympathy becomes
disfigured in the eyes of whites so that it appears as little more than an
indicator of self-perceived advantage on the part of the black subject. A
black man is not allowed to have feelings that imply a position of any
¹⁶ Harper Lee, To Kill a Mockingbird (London: William Heinemann, 1960), 202.
¹⁷ Ibid. 201.

Recap
Green’s theorem in a plane, positive direction of boundary of a region in the 2D
Conservative field and potential, simply connected regions
Surface integrals, direction of a surface and its perimeter, vector area of a surface in
terms of line integral along its perimeter
Relation between (i) a = Ñf in region R and (ii) Ñ×a = 0.
Previously we concluded that (i) and (ii) are equivalent when R is simply connected.
More precisely, we have
• a = Ñf in R implies Ñ×a = 0, regardless of the connectivity of R.
• Ñ×a = 0 implies a = Ñf in R only when R is simply connected.
Terminology:
a = Ñf (in a region R) is called a conservative field (a non-local property).
Ñ×a = 0 (in a region R) is called an irrotational field (a local property).
Comments:
• Being an irrotational field is a local property.
Suppose vector field a is irrotational both in region A and in region B. Then a is
irrotational in region (AÈB).
• Being a conservative field is a non-local property.
Suppose vector field a is conservative both in region A and in region B. This,
however, does not imply that a is conservative in region (AÈB). When we try to
piece together the two potentials Φ
A 
and Φ
B
, the resulting function may not be
single-valued and continuous in (AÈB).

Notation: A number of the form z = x + i y is called a complex number.
Re(z) = x, the real part of z.
Im(z) = y, the imaginary part of z.
Complex plane and Argand diagram
There is a one-to-one correspondence between all complex numbers and all points (all
vectors) in the xy-plane, which is called the complex plane.
z = x + i y is also denoted by z = (x, y)
A geometric representation of one or more complex numbers in the complex plane is
called an Argand diagram (named after Jean-Robert Argand).
