name: Homework 4
description: This is the last homework for my CSE 101 Homework and it doesn't seem that hard to implement BST honestly

docs.content:
HWK4 - CSE101
Due before midnight, Saturday, May 31, 2025.
Late submissions will not be accepted/graded.

Changelog:
1. Objectives:
Implement a Binary Search Tree (BST) and support the following functionalities:
Construct the BST by inserting elements in the given order
Perform insertions, deletions, search, and traversals (inorder, preorder, postorder)
Note: For consistency of output, do not implement any self-balancing mechanism (such as AVL or Red-Black Tree rotations). Your BST should be constructed exactly in the order of insertions, resulting in a standard unbalanced BST. This ensures your output can be directly compared to the reference output using diff.
2. Description:
In this assignment, you will implement a full application that manages a binary search tree (BST). You will write both the BST logic and the main program logic that reads input, interprets commands, and interacts with the BST.
The application will:

Build the initial BST by inserting elements one by one in input order
Support operations to insert, delete, find, and traverse the BST
Read input from standard input and print output to standard output
3. Input Format:
The first line contains a single integer n - the number of elements in the initial array.
The second line contains n space-separated integers - the elements to be inserted in order into the BST.
Each subsequent line contains a command (one per line) until the exit command is encountered. Subsequent lines contain commands such as:
      
        insert x
        delete x
        find x
        inorder
        preorder
        postorder
        exit
      
    
4. Supported Commands:
Command	Description
insert x	Insert a node with value x into the BST
delete x	Delete the node with value x from the BST. When deleting a node with two children, your program must replace it with its inorder successor - i.e., the smallest value in the node's right subtree.
If you try to delete a value not present in the BST, your program should do nothing (i.e., the tree remains unchanged and no output is produced).

find x	Print Found (Depth: d) or Not found for the value x. (root is depth 0)
inorder	Print BST in inorder traversal
preorder	Print BST in preorder traversal
postorder	Print BST in postorder traversal
exit	Terminate the program
Note:

You may ignore duplicate insertions (no need to insert the same value twice). If you meet duplicate insertions, do nothing (no duplicates).
Output for traversals should be a single space-separated line with a newline at the end.
Output for find should be exactly Found (Depth: d) or Not found.
5. Example input and output
Given this test1.in. The program outputs test1.out.
Your output should be able to pass "diff" test.

test1.in:

      
        5
        10 5 15 3 8
        find 8
        find 20
        inorder
        preorder
        postorder
        delete 5
        inorder
        exit
      
    
test1.out
      
        Found (Depth: 2)
        Not found
        3 5 8 10 15 
        10 5 3 8 15 
        3 8 5 15 10 
        3 8 10 15 
      
    
6. Files Provided
BST.h - Provided. Contains function declarations and the BST node structure. Do not change this file.
BST.c - You implement this. Contains all BST logic (insert, delete, find, traversal).
hwk4.c - Main application file. You implement this. You write the code to read input and process commands.
test1.in - example input for testing.
Makefile - Provided. Compile using make to generate the executable hwk4. Do not change this file.
test1.out - example output for test1.in.
Hints:

Implement createNode, insertNode, deleteNode, findNode, inorderTraversal, preorderTraversal, postorderTraversal, and freeTree in BST.c
In hwk4.c, read the input using scanf or fgets, parse commands with strcmp, and call the appropriate functions
Use Makefile to compile: make
Run it with: ./hwk4 < test1.in
7. Rubric
    You start off with 100 points.
    You lose credit for missing functionality, incorrect results, poorly documented or formatted code, or not following instructions.
    Below is a partial list of deductions:
    - Up to 10 points off for inadequate comments or hard-to-read code.
    - Up to 10 points off for requiring special handling to grade your homework
     (usually because you did not test that it runs on the Unix timeshare first).
    - Up to 10 points off for not following submission instructions (e.g., improper zip file format).
    - Your submission is tested on 5 test cases. Each test case evaluates the correctness of your implementation. Partial credit is awarded per feature within each test case (e.g., traversal, find, delete).
        - If your program crashes or exits early for a test case, no points are awarded for that case unless partial output matches.
        - You are responsible for freeing memory at the end. Repeated memory leaks or misuse may result in up to 5 points off.
    
    Make sure you:
    a. include a Makefile, all .c files and .h files listed above.
    b. generate an executable file called hwk4 with your makefile
    c. have confirmed that it compiles and runs properly on unix.ucsc.edu
    d. followed the general instructions described in overview.html
    
  
8. Submission:
In addition to comparing your program's output with the provided example (test1.out), be sure to unit test your individual functions. And it's recommended that you design additional test cases to check edge cases. The test cases used for grading will not be released.
Make sure you have compiled and tested your code on the campus Unix timeshare before submitting.

#ifndef VOCAB_BST_H
#define VOCAB_BST_H

#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>

// Structure representing a node in the AVL tree (or basic BST)
typedef struct TokenNode {
    char* token;                   // The string token (e.g., "apple")
    int token_id;                 // Unique ID assigned to the token
    struct TokenNode* left;      // Pointer to the left child
    struct TokenNode* right;     // Pointer to the right child

    // Extra fields for AVL bonus, optional to use for prog4_basic
    struct TokenNode* parent;    // Pointer to parent node (optional)
    int height;                  // Height of the node (for AVL balancing)
    int balanceFactor;           // Balance factor: left height - right height
} TokenNode;

/**
 * Create a new node with the given token and token_id.
 * Initializes left/right/parent pointers and AVL-related fields.
 * 
 * @param token      The string token to store in the node.
 * @param token_id   The unique integer ID of the token.
 * @return           Pointer to the newly created node.
 */
TokenNode* createNode(const char* token, int token_id);

/**
 * Insert a token into the BST or AVL tree.
 * If the token already exists, no changes are made.
 * 
 * @param root       Pointer to the root of the tree.
 * @param token      Token to insert.
 * @param token_id   ID to assign if the token is new.
 * @param inserted   Output flag (1 if inserted, 0 if duplicate).
 * @return           Updated root of the tree.
 */
TokenNode* insertToken(TokenNode* root, const char* token, int token_id, int* inserted);

/**
 * Delete a token from the BST or AVL tree.
 * If the token does not exist, the tree remains unchanged.
 * If the node has two children, replaces it with its inorder successor.
 * Frees memory associated with the token string and the node.
 * 
 * @param root       Pointer to the root of the tree.
 * @param token      Token to delete.
 * @param deleted    Output flag (1 if deleted, 0 if not found).
 * @return           Updated root of the tree.
 */
TokenNode* deleteToken(TokenNode* root, const char* token, int* deleted);

/**
 * Search for a token in the tree and report its ID and depth if found.
 * 
 * @param root           Pointer to the root of the tree.
 * @param token          Token to search for.
 * @param token_id_out   Output: ID of the token if found.
 * @param depth_out      Output: Depth of the node (root is depth 0).
 * @return               Pointer to the found node, or NULL if not found.
 */
TokenNode* findToken(TokenNode* root, const char* token, int* token_id_out, int* depth_out);

/**
 * Print the tokens in lexicographic (inorder) order.
 * 
 * @param root       Pointer to the root of the tree.
 */
void inorderPrint(TokenNode* root);

/**
 * Print the tokens in preorder (Root, Left, Right) order.
 * 
 * @param root       Pointer to the root of the tree.
 */
void preorderPrint(TokenNode* root);

/**
 * Print the tokens in postorder (Left, Right, Root) order.
 * 
 * @param root       Pointer to the root of the tree.
 */
void postorderPrint(TokenNode* root);

/**
 * Free all memory associated with the tree.
 * 
 * @param root       Double pointer to the root. Sets *root to NULL after freeing.
 */
void freeTree(TokenNode** root);

#endif

5
</w> a are hello world
preorder
insert apple
preorder
insert hello
preorder
find are
delete world
preorder
inorder
postorder
exit

prog4: prog4.o VocabBST.o
	gcc -o prog4 prog4.o VocabBST.o

prog4.o: prog4.c VocabBST.h
	gcc -c prog4.c

VocabBST.o: VocabBST.c VocabBST.h
	gcc -c VocabBST.c

clean:
	rm -f *.o prog4

</w> a are hello world 
Inserted: apple (id=5)
</w> a are apple hello world 
Token already exists
</w> a are apple hello world 
Found: are (id=2) (Depth: 2)
Deleted: world
</w> a are apple hello 
</w> a apple are hello 
apple hello are a </w>

PROG4 - CSE101
Due before midnight, Saturday, June 7, 2025.
Late submissions will not be accepted/graded.

Changelog:
1. Objectives:
In this assignment, you will:
Implement a Binary Search Tree (BST) that stores a lexicographically ordered vocabulary of tokens.
Support the following operations:
Insert new tokens (lexicographic order)
Delete tokens
Search for tokens (and report their id and depth)
Print tokens using inorder, preorder, and postorder traversals
Optionally (bonus) convert your BST into a self-balancing AVL Tree
2. Description:
You will implement a BST to manage a vocabulary of tokens. Your BST will store (token, token_id) pairs in lexicographic order (based on token string), allowing for insertion, deletion, search, and traversal. The vocabulary will initially be built from a sorted list of tokens read from stdin. (The tree doesn't need to be balanced for the basic part of prog4)
2.1 Problem
You will maintain a BST where each node stores a pair: (token: string, token_id: int)
Token strings are lowercase words with no whitespace. Refer to hwk3.
Each token receives a unique token_id starting from 0. 0 for the first token, 1 for the second, and so on. Refer to hwk3.
The tree does not need to be balanced (unless you're doing the bonus).
2.2 Initial Vocabulary
The first line of input is an integer n, representing the number of initial tokens.
The next line contains n space-separated tokens.
These tokens are inserted into the tree in the order given, with token_id starting from 0.
2.3 Supported Commands
After the initial vocabulary is read, your program will process commands (one per line):
Command	Description
insert x	Insert token x. Assign next token_id. Print result. Ignore duplicates.
delete x	Delete token x from the BST. When deleting a node with two children, your program must replace it with its inorder successor - i.e., the smallest value in the node's right subtree.
If you try to delete a value not present in the BST, your program should do nothing (i.e., the tree remains unchanged and no output is produced).

find x	Print Found: x (id=ID) (Depth: D) or Not found for the token x for the value x. (root is depth 0)
inorder	Print BST in inorder traversal (lexicographic)
preorder	Print BST in preorder traversal
postorder	Print BST in postorder traversal
exit	Exit the program and free memory
Note:

Ignore duplicate insertions (no need to insert the same value twice). If you meet duplicate insertions, do nothing (no duplicates).
Output for traversals should be a single space-separated line with a newline at the end.
Output for find x should be exactly Found: x (id=ID) (Depth: D) or Not found for the token x.
3. Example input and output
Given this test1.in. The program outputs test1.out.basic.
Given this test2.in. The program outputs test2.out.basic.

Your output should be able to pass "diff" test.

test1.in:

      
        5
        </w> a are hello world
        preorder
        insert apple
        preorder
        insert hello
        preorder
        find are
        delete world
        preorder
        inorder
        postorder
        exit
      
    
test1.out.basic
      
        </w> a are hello world 
        Inserted: apple (id=5)
        </w> a are apple hello world 
        Token already exists
        </w> a are apple hello world 
        Found: are (id=2) (Depth: 2)
        Deleted: world
        </w> a are apple hello 
        </w> a apple are hello 
        apple hello are a </w> 
      
    
test2.in:
      
        7
        mango apple peach banana cherry date fig
        preorder
        insert apricot
        preorder
        insert banana
        preorder
        find cherry
        find durian
        delete peach
        inorder
        preorder
        postorder
        exit
      
    
test2.out.basic
      
        mango apple banana cherry date fig peach 
        Inserted: apricot (id=7)
        mango apple banana apricot cherry date fig peach 
        Token already exists
        mango apple banana apricot cherry date fig peach 
        Found: cherry (id=4) (Depth: 3)
        Not found for the token durian
        Deleted: peach
        apple apricot banana cherry date fig mango 
        mango apple banana apricot cherry date fig 
        apricot fig date cherry banana apple mango 
      
    
4. Files Provided
VocabBST.h - BST header. Defines TokenNode and all BST function prototypes. Do not change this file.
VocabBST.c - You implement this. Contains all BST logic (insert, delete, find, traversal). No need to balance the tree.
prog4.c - Main application file. You implement this. You write the code to read input and process commands.
test1.in - example input for testing.
test2.in - example input for testing.
Makefile - Provided. Compile using make to generate the executable prog4. Do not change this file.
test1.out.basic - example output for test1.in.
test2.out.basic - example output for test2.in.
Hints:

Use Makefile to compile: make
Run it with: ./prog4 < test1.in
5. Bonus (Up to +15 points): AVL Tree Implementation
You must complete the basic part of prog4 before you can get bonus points. If your basic part score is less than 80, your bonus part will not be scored.
5.1 Objective
Implement a balanced version of your token BST using AVL tree logic.
5.2 Files
bonus.c - Bonus version of the main program (identical behavior to prog4.c)
VocabAVL.h - AVL version of the header file. Includes balance-related fields. Do not change this file.
VocabAVL.c - AVL version of the tree logic. You must implement insert/delete using rotations.
5.3 What You Need to Do
Implement AVL logic: track node height, calculate balance factor, and perform rotations (LL, RR, LR, RL) as needed.
Behavior must exactly match the basic version in terms of input/output format.
5.4 Tips
Use the height and balanceFactor fields to maintain balance.
Maintain parent pointers to simplify re-linking during rotations.
5.5 Bonus Test Example
Your output should be able to pass "diff" test.
Example input1 test1.in: Same as '3. Example Input / Output'

Example output1 (Bonus with AVL): test1.out.bonus

      
        a </w> hello are world 
        Inserted: apple (id=5)
        are a </w> apple hello world 
        Token already exists
        are a </w> apple hello world 
        Found: are (id=2) (Depth: 0)
        Deleted: world
        are a </w> apple hello 
        </w> a apple are hello 
        </w> apple a hello are 
      
    
Example input2 test2.in: Same as '3. Example Input / Output'
Example output2 (Bonus with AVL): test2.out.bonus

      
        cherry banana apple mango date fig peach 
        Inserted: apricot (id=7)
        cherry apricot apple banana mango date fig peach 
        Token already exists
        cherry apricot apple banana mango date fig peach 
        Found: cherry (id=4) (Depth: 0)
        Not found for the token durian
        Deleted: peach
        apple apricot banana cherry date fig mango 
        cherry apricot apple banana fig date mango 
        apple banana apricot date mango fig cherry 
      
    
6. Rubric
For the basic part:
    You start off with 100 points.
    You lose credit for missing functionality, incorrect results, poorly documented or formatted code, or not following instructions.
    Below is a partial list of deductions:
    - Up to 10 points off for inadequate comments or hard-to-read code.
    - Up to 10 points off for requiring special handling to grade your homework
     (usually because you did not test that it runs on the Unix timeshare first).
    - Up to 10 points off for not following submission instructions (e.g., improper zip file format).
    - Your submission is tested on 3 test cases. Each test case evaluates the correctness of your implementation. Partial credit is awarded per feature within each test case (e.g., traversal, find, delete).
        - If your program crashes or exits early for a test case, no points are awarded for that case unless partial output matches.
        - You are responsible for freeing memory at the end. Repeated memory leaks or misuse may result in up to 5 points off.
    
    Make sure you:
    a. include a Makefile, all .c files and .h files listed above.
    b. generate an executable file called prog4 with your makefile
    c. have confirmed that it compiles and runs properly on unix.ucsc.edu
    d. followed the general instructions described in overview.html
    
  
For the bonus part
there are 15 points for the bonus part. Grading rubric is similar to the basic part.
7. Submission:
In addition to comparing your program's output with the provided example (test1.out.basic), be sure to unit test your individual functions. And it's recommended that you design additional test cases to check edge cases. The test cases used for grading will not be released.
Make sure you have compiled and tested your code on the campus Unix timeshare before submitting.

Put your makefile and source files in a folder named prog4.

In the same directory where you have the prog4 folder, the following command will recursively archive the contents of your prog4 folder into a file named prog4.zip
zip -r prog4.zip prog4
Submit prog4.zip on Canvas under prog4.

name: Solving the spam call problem
description: I just want people to stop getting spam calls

docs.filename: Spam calling which is a multibillion dollar problem
docs.content:
Great question - the verification process is where the rubber meets the road for digital signatures. Here's how it would work technically:

## **Certificate Chain Verification**

**Root certificate authorities**: Each country would have government-backed telecom certificate authorities (like how web browsers trust certain root CAs). The US FCC, UK Ofcom, etc. would operate or designate these authorities.

**Carrier certificates**: Each legitimate telecom carrier gets a certificate from their national CA that proves they're authorized to originate calls from specific number ranges. This certificate includes:
- Carrier identity and licensing information
- Which number ranges they're authorized to use
- Cryptographic public key for signature verification

**Chain of trust**: When a call crosses borders, the receiving carrier verifies:
1. The signature was created by a valid carrier certificate
2. That carrier is authorized for the claimed phone number
3. The certificate chains back to a trusted national authority

## **Real-Time Verification Process**

**SIP header extensions**: The digital signature would be embedded in extended SIP headers alongside the call setup data. The signature would cover:
- The claimed caller ID number
- Timestamp to prevent replay attacks
- The originating carrier's identity
- A hash of the call routing path

**Gateway verification**: When a call hits a US international gateway:
1. Extract the digital signature from call headers
2. Look up the signing carrier's certificate 
3. Verify the signature matches the claimed caller ID
4. Check if that carrier is authorized for that number range
5. Validate the certificate hasn't been revoked

## **The Hard Part: International Coordination**

**Cross-border certificate recognition**: This is the biggest challenge. You'd need:
- International treaties recognizing each country's telecom CAs
- Standardized certificate formats and verification protocols
- Real-time certificate revocation lists shared globally
- Dispute resolution for certificate validation conflicts

**Fallback for non-participating countries**: Calls from countries without digital signature systems would need to be:
- Clearly marked as "unverified international"
- Subject to higher blocking thresholds
- Potentially charged premium authentication fees (your original idea)

## **Implementation Challenges**

**Legacy system integration**: Older phone switches don't support extended SIP headers, so you'd need:
- Gateway translation services
- Gradual migration periods
- Backward compatibility modes

**Certificate management**: Managing millions of certificates across global carriers requires:
- Automated certificate issuance and renewal
- Real-time revocation capabilities
- Secure key storage and rotation

**Performance impact**: Signature verification adds latency to call setup, requiring:
- Optimized cryptographic algorithms
- Cached certificate validation
- Parallel processing of signature checks

The most realistic approach might be starting with a smaller coalition of countries (US, Canada, EU) implementing mutual certificate recognition, then expanding globally over time. Countries that don't participate would see their calls treated as "unverified" and subject to much stricter filtering.

What's your sense of whether the international coordination aspect is the fatal flaw, or if there's a way to make it work incrementally?

It seems that there is a large number of spam calls in the united states, there seems to be a lot of different ways of doing spam though through phone calls, email, text messages, social media platforms like Facebook, Youtube, Instagram, Linkedin, where they always try to redirect you off platform.

name: the king of AV1
description: I want to put into effect AV1 encoding for HBO Max, Disney Plus, Peacock, Paramout +, Amazon Prime Video and Apple Tv+.

docs.content: I am a kid who wants to implement AV1 at the large streaming companies who currently don't use it. Namely HBO max, Disney Plus, Peacock, Paramount +, Amazon Prime Video, and Apple TV+. I want to understand what I would make during the summer to demonstrate that I understand the core concepts behind what is required for their infrastructure to implement this stuff in less than 6 months.

docs.filename: Parts of the Instrastructure that I need to build
docs.content:
Encoding Infrastructure
Hardware/Software Encoders: You'll need AV1 encoders that can handle the company's content volume. This includes understanding both software encoders (like SVT-AV1, libaom) and hardware acceleration options (Intel QSV, NVIDIA NVENC with AV1 support, dedicated AV1 chips).
Encoding Pipeline Integration: How to integrate AV1 encoding into existing transcoding workflows, including multi-bitrate encoding for adaptive streaming and how to handle the significantly longer encoding times compared to H.264/HEVC.
Content Delivery Network (CDN)
Storage Requirements: AV1 files alongside existing H.264/HEVC versions (initially you'll need both for compatibility), understanding storage cost implications.
CDN Distribution: Ensuring CDN providers can efficiently cache and serve AV1 content, and understanding bandwidth savings potential (typically 20-30% over HEVC).
Player Integration
Decoder Support: Understanding which devices/browsers support AV1 hardware decoding vs software decoding, and the performance implications.
Adaptive Bitrate Logic: Modifying existing ABR algorithms to intelligently choose between AV1, HEVC, and H.264 based on device capabilities and network conditions.
Fallback Mechanisms: Ensuring seamless fallback to HEVC/H.264 for devices without AV1 support.
Quality Control & Testing
Quality Metrics: Understanding how to measure AV1 quality using metrics like VMAF, PSNR, and SSIM, and establishing quality targets that match or exceed current HEVC output.
Device Testing Matrix: Testing across different devices, operating systems, and browsers to ensure consistent playback experience.
Infrastructure Scaling
Processing Power Requirements: AV1 encoding is computationally intensive - understanding the infrastructure scaling needed for your content volume.
Cost-Benefit Analysis: Demonstrating bandwidth savings vs increased encoding costs and storage requirements during transition period.
For a summer demonstration, I'd recommend focusing on building a proof-of-concept pipeline that shows encoding a sample piece of content to AV1, serving it through a simple CDN setup, and playing it back with quality measurements - this would demonstrate you understand the full technical stack involved.

docs.filename: The current idea for the project
docs.content:
Base Goal (3 Components - MVP)

AV1 Encoding Pipeline - Basic SVT-AV1/libaom integration with multi-bitrate output
Storage & Organization - File management system that handles AV1 alongside existing formats
Quality Measurement - VMAF/PSNR comparison dashboard showing AV1 vs HEVC/H.264 results

This base version proves you understand the core technical requirements and can deliver a working system. It's something you could realistically complete in a summer and would still be impressive.
Expanded Goals (Add Advanced Components)
Then you can layer on the enterprise-scale challenges:

Distributed Encoding Orchestration - Scale from single-machine to encoding farm
Cost Optimization Engine - Add intelligent decision-making about when to use AV1
Advanced Quality Control - Automated testing and ML-based optimization
Enterprise Monitoring - Real-time dashboards and predictive analytics

This approach has several advantages:

You have something to show even if you don't finish everything
You can prioritize which advanced features matter most for the companies you're targeting
It demonstrates both technical execution (working base system) and strategic thinking (understanding enterprise complexity)
You can continue expanding it beyond the summer if it goes well

name: Rivendell
description: An open source palantir clone that is putting both snowflake and palantir in the ground

docs.content:
Technical architecture foundation
Core platform architecture requirements
Based on Palantir Foundry's proven approach, the open source alternative should implement a microservices architecture with these essential layers:
Infrastructure Layer: Kubernetes-native containerized services with auto-scaling capabilities powered by established patterns from successful platforms. Loft The foundation requires container orchestration through Kubernetes, with service mesh implementation using Istio or Linkerd for secure inter-service communication.
Storage Layer: Multi-modal storage architecture supporting blob storage, HDFS, horizontally scalable key-value stores, and relational databases. Apache Iceberg emerges as the optimal choice for the table format, providing ACID transactions, schema evolution, and vendor-neutral compatibility across processing engines. DremioStarburst
Compute Layer: Pluggable compute engines supporting Apache Spark as the primary processing engine for batch workloads, Apache Flink for streaming requirements, and Ray for ML-intensive applications. This multi-engine approach matches Foundry's compute-agnostic framework. Datafold
Semantic Layer: Central metadata and ontology management system serving as the operational representation of enterprise data relationships. This proves critical for replicating Foundry's unique data integration capabilities.
Security Layer: Zero-trust architecture with comprehensive RBAC, audit logging, and compliance features enforced at every tier. Intuitive +2 Keycloak provides enterprise-grade identity management, while Open Policy Agent (OPA) enables fine-grained authorization policies.

What does {Startup} do? 

How would I use {startup} to help me?

# YC Startup Research Report: 123 Companies Analyzed

Based on comprehensive research from official YC profiles, company websites, and recent announcements, here's detailed information on 123 Y Combinator startups covering what each company does and how you could use their products or services.

## Comprehensive Startup Directory

### A

**Almond (Women's Health)**
- **What it does:** Modernizes OB-GYN care through tech-enabled clinics and telehealth, offering pregnancy planning, birth control counseling, and sexual health services
- **How you'd use it:** Book OB-GYN appointments with shorter wait times, access both telehealth and in-person care, get subscription-based comprehensive women's health services ($250 annually)

**Anvil (SEO Platform)**
- **What it does:** SEO platform for the "LLM era" helping brands optimize visibility in AI-powered search tools like ChatGPT, Gemini, and Perplexity
- **How you'd use it:** Track brand performance across AI search tools, optimize content for AI-generated responses, monitor competitors in AI search results

**Approval AI**
- **What it does:** AI-powered mortgage co-pilot that automates rate shopping, negotiation, and paperwork for homebuyers
- **How you'd use it:** Get better mortgage rates through AI negotiation, avoid spam calls while shopping for loans, automate complex mortgage paperwork

**Aravolta**
- **What it does:** Comprehensive data center monitoring and management software with real-time monitoring and predictive analytics
- **How you'd use it:** Monitor data center infrastructure health, optimize power usage efficiency, track compliance with energy standards

**Atlog**
- **What it does:** Voice agents for physical retail stores, targeting rent-to-own furniture stores for sales and collections calls
- **How you'd use it:** Automate 200+ daily sales and collection calls, recover outstanding payments, reduce staff overhead in retail operations

**Attune**
- **What it does:** Ultra-fast build tools requiring zero migration - provides drop-in replacements for existing build commands with up to 20x speed improvements
- **How you'd use it:** Dramatically reduce build times by prefixing existing commands with "hurry" (e.g., "hurry cargo build"), deploy code faster without workflow changes

**Auctor**
- **What it does:** AI agents that streamline enterprise software implementations by automating requirements capture and documentation generation
- **How you'd use it:** Reduce implementation timelines from weeks to hours, avoid costly change orders in enterprise software projects

**Avallon AI**

- **How you'd use it:** Focus on decision-making instead of data entry as an adjuster, reduce claim resolution time, provide 24/7 customer service

### B

- **How you'd use it:** Automate creator finances, optimize taxes, manage multi-source income streams, transform from spreadsheets to professional business operations

**Besimple AI**

- **How you'd use it:** Create annotation interfaces in 60 seconds, reduce labeling costs with AI automation, handle multimodal training data efficiently

**Better Auth**

## Summary

This comprehensive analysis covers 123 Y Combinator startups, with successful research completed for 120 companies. Three companies (Crimson, HABIT, Clidely) could not be confirmed as YC startups based on available information.

**Key Trends Identified:**
- **AI-First Solutions:** 95% of companies leverage AI as core technology
- **Enterprise Focus:** Majority target B2B customers with automation solutions
- **Industry Specialization:** Companies address specific verticals (healthcare, legal, manufacturing, etc.)
- **Productivity Enhancement:** Common theme of eliminating manual work and improving efficiency
- **Recent Vintage:** Most companies founded 2024-2025, representing latest YC cohorts

**Most Common Use Cases:**
1. **Automation of repetitive tasks** across various industries
2. **AI-powered data analysis** and insights generation
3. **Workflow optimization** and process improvement
4. **Developer tools** and infrastructure solutions
5. **Customer service enhancement** through AI agents

All information was gathered from official Y Combinator profiles, company websites, and verified startup databases, ensuring accuracy and currency of the research findings.

AM160 Final Exam
1 Problem 1 (15 points)
In this problem, we are going to work with real weather data. The link to the
dataset is: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1MLsBsj869ER87JZSo6jttap4hdvF236q?
usp=sharing.
There are 5 datasets, each of them are NETCDF4 files. They are labeled
as ”z1979.nc”, ”z1979.nc”, ..., corresponding to years, 1979, 1980, etc. For this
problem, you are required to do the following and write a report on it:
(a) Load the data files, consider the ”z” variable which would have shape
time × channel × latitude × longitude. The data is sampled every 6 hours.
Plot the spatial patterns at a few time steps from any of the years and present
them as contour plots (google around and see how spatial plots like these are
presented using matplotlib)
(b) Use the first 4 years of the data as training data and the last year
as testing data. You may choose to use less to avoid memory overload, but
remember that your model would suffer from inaccuracies. You are going to
train a denoising variational autoencoder. In order to do that:
• First, corrupt your training data. To corrupt it, add sparsity to the spatial
points for each of the temporal samples. Suppose, your spatial data is
given by X(t), where X is the channel × latitude × longitude tensor at
each time, t. Choose k random points in the latitude × longitude domain
and make them 0 (this is called sparsity). Call this corrputed data as
X
c
(t). For every time sample in the training data over 4 years, make sure
that you are sparsifying a random set of k points.
• Train a convolutional variational autoencoder that maps X
c
(t) to X(t).
• From the testing dataset (1985), sparsify the data again and see how well
you reconstruct the full dense samples at different time steps.
• Perform experiments where k is 30%, 60%, and 90% of the total number
of grid points on each channel.
• You may choose to input the random mask that sparsifies each sample. It
may improve your performance.
Your report must be easily understandable as to what you exactly did, how
you trained your model, and how your experimental results look like.
1

2 Problem 2 (15 points)
In this problem, you are going to use the same dataset as Problem 1. However,
here, you are going to perform autoregressive prediction, just like we did for the
Lorenz 63 system. Here, you will write a conditional generative model (either
a VAE or a diffusion model) to predict X(t + 1) from X(t). Train your model,
similar to how we did in class (for Lorenz 63) to train a model that moves
forward by one time step. Key points to remember:
• Your generative model would take X(t + 1) as input and the label would
also be X(t + 1)
• X(t) is concatenated with the latent space (in case of a VAE) or with the
Gaussian noise sample (for diffusion model)
• When you show prediction results for year 1985, start from the first time
step (Jan 1), and autoregressively predict the next few days.
• Plot a few predicted spatial patterns for a few days and the corresponding
true patterns for both the channels.
• Plot the MSE error between the predicted and true labels for each channel
separately as a function of time.
3 Problem 3 (10 points)
The forward diffusion process in a diffusion model is given by the process
q (x
t
|x
t−1
):
x(t) = 
p
(1 − β
t
)x(t − 1) + 
p
β
t
N (0, I) (1)
Prove that q would converge to a unit Gaussian as t → ∞ if β
t 
is constant
in time.
2
